chunk_summary: Outpatient CMAM programs in India define recovery criteria based on anthropometric measures and set maximum treatment durations (12–16 weeks) with referral protocols for non-responders.
learning_objectives: Understand outpatient recovery criteria for SAM management, including MUAC and oedema-free standards.
Learn the importance of consistent anthropometric monitoring during outpatient treatment.
Identify triggers for non-response referral and structured evaluations in CMAM programs.
Explore Indian state protocols for maximum treatment duration in outpatient SAM management.
key_facts: Recovery criteria include MUAC ≥125 mm or WHZ ≥-2 with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least 2 weeks.
Maximum outpatient treatment duration is typically 12–16 weeks in Indian CMAM programs.
Non-response triggers include weight gain <5 g/kg/day over several weeks, persistent oedema, or appetite-test failure.
Percentage weight gain is not recommended as a sole recovery criterion due to bias from admission weight.
Structured evaluations for non-responders should investigate missed infections, chronic diarrhea, or malabsorption.
reference_quote: A widely used WHO criterion for discharge from nutritional treatment is: MUAC ≥125 mm with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least 2 weeks, or WHZ ≥-2 with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least 2 weeks.
document_key_topics: Outpatient recovery criteria for SAM management
Maximum treatment duration and referral protocols
